night_routine: Double cleanse: oil cleanser → water-based cleanser
Retinol serum (0.3%) — start every other night
dos: Stay hydrated — at least 2 liters of water daily
donts: Don't skip sunscreen — UV darkens marks
Don't use harsh scrubs on active breakouts

Oily: Use an oil-free Salicylic Acid (2%) cleanser morning and night.
Apply a lightweight, mattifying moisturizer with SPF.
Use clay masks (Kaolin) once a week to absorb excess oil.
Avoid heavy makeup — switch to mineral or oil-free foundation.
Blotting papers during the day can help control shine.
Dry: Use a gentle, cream-based cleanser (CeraVe Hydrating).
Apply Hyaluronic Acid serum before moisturizer for deep hydration.
Use a mild BHA exfoliant (0.5% Salicylic Acid) 2x/week only.
Rich moisturizer with Ceramides to repair skin barrier.
Avoid hot water when washing — it strips moisture.
Combination: Use Salicylic Acid on your T-zone only (nose, forehead, chin).
Apply a light gel moisturizer on oily zones, cream on dry zones.
Multi-masking: clay mask on T-zone, hydrating mask on cheeks.
Sensitive: Avoid Salicylic Acid — use Azelaic Acid (10%) instead.
Patch test all new products on your inner arm first.
Centella Asiatica (Cica) cream helps soothe and heal.
Minimize skincare steps — less is more for sensitive skin.
Normal: Use a gentle foaming cleanser with Salicylic Acid.
Apply a light moisturizer with SPF 30+.
Exfoliate once a week with a BHA or AHA product.
Change pillowcases every 2-3 days to prevent bacteria buildup.

Blackheads: Pore strips can give instant relief but aren't a long-term fix.
Retinol at night prevents new blackheads from forming.
Whiteheads: Gentle chemical exfoliation with AHA (Glycolic Acid).
Never squeeze — it pushes bacteria deeper and causes scarring.

morning_routine: Spot treatment on active pimples if needed
night_routine: Gentle cleanser — no actives (to avoid over-drying)
Adapalene (Differin 0.1%) on clean skin — every night
Wait 15 min, then apply moisturizer with Ceramides
dos: Be consistent — stick to routine for 8 full weeks
Ice inflamed pimples for 5 min to reduce swelling
Introduce new actives one at a time (purging is normal)
Keep your hands off your face during the day
donts: Don't mix Benzoyl Peroxide with Retinoids at the same time
Don't pop pustules — it spreads bacteria and causes scars
Don't use more than 5% BP — higher % just irritates more
Don't give up after 2 weeks — actives need 6–8 weeks to work

Oily: Use Benzoyl Peroxide (5%) wash in the morning.
Apply Retinoid (Adapalene 0.1%) at night on clean skin.
Niacinamide (10%) serum controls oil AND reduces inflammation.
Zinc supplements (30mg/day) can help reduce oil production.
Dry: Use Benzoyl Peroxide (2.5%) — lower concentration to avoid drying.
Apply Retinoid only every other night, always with moisturizer.
Use Hyaluronic Acid serum to counteract drying effects of treatments.
Squalane oil is non-comedogenic and deeply hydrating.
Avoid foaming cleansers — use cream or milk cleansers.
Combination: Apply Benzoyl Peroxide spot treatment on inflamed areas only.
Use Retinoid on oily/acne-prone zones, skip dry areas.
Niacinamide works great for combination skin — balances oil.
Lightweight gel moisturizer with Aloe Vera.
Sensitive: Skip Benzoyl Peroxide — use Azelaic Acid (15%) instead.
Green tea extract reduces inflammation without irritation.
Aloe Vera gel as moisturizer to calm redness.
If irritation persists, see a dermatologist for prescription options.
Normal: Benzoyl Peroxide (2.5-5%) wash or spot treatment.
Consistent routine for 6-8 weeks before expecting results.
Avoid touching your face during the day.

Papules: Benzoyl Peroxide kills bacteria causing the inflammation.
Azelaic Acid reduces redness and swelling.
Ice the area for 5 minutes to reduce swelling quickly.
Pustules: Let pustules drain naturally — do NOT squeeze.
Tea tree oil (diluted 5%) has natural antibacterial properties.

morning_routine: Azelaic Acid (15%) — reduces redness without irritation
SPF 50+ sunscreen — mandatory to prevent scarring
night_routine: Gentle cleanse — no scrubs, no actives
Prescription treatment as directed by dermatologist
Lip balm with SPF — if on Isotretinoin
dos: See a dermatologist — prescription treatment is essential
Drink 3+ liters of water daily (especially on Isotretinoin)
Take before/after photos monthly to track progress
Be patient — severe acne treatment takes 3–6 months
donts: NEVER squeeze or pick at cysts — causes permanent scars
Don't self-treat severe acne with OTC products alone
Don't use harsh exfoliants or scrubs

Oily: See a dermatologist — you likely need Isotretinoin (Accutane).
Oral antibiotics (Doxycycline) can reduce bacterial infection.
Use a gentle cleanser — aggressive products make it worse.
Oil-free SPF is crucial — sun exposure darkens acne scars.
Consider Spironolactone if hormonal (common in women).
Dry: Dermatologist visit is essential — prescription treatments needed.
If prescribed Isotretinoin, expect extreme dryness — use Aquaphor.
CeraVe Moisturizing Cream is a must during treatment.
Drink extra water — at least 3 liters/day during treatment.
Lip balm with SPF — lips will crack on Isotretinoin.
Combination: Consult a dermatologist for a combination approach.
Oral + topical treatment usually works best for combo skin.
Gentle cleansing is key — avoid any harsh scrubs.
Sensitive: See a dermatologist immediately — do NOT self-treat severe acne.
Sensitive skin + severe acne = prescription-only approach.
Extremely gentle products only: Vanicream or La Roche-Posay Toleriane.
Cortisone injections can help flatten painful cysts quickly.
Avoid all DIY treatments — they can cause permanent damage.
Normal: Isotretinoin is the gold standard for severe cystic acne.
Treatment takes 3-6 months — patience is key.
Avoid picking or squeezing — it causes permanent scarring.
Maintain a low-inflammation diet: Omega-3, vegetables, water.

Cystic Acne: Cortisone injections flatten cysts within 24 hours.
Isotretinoin (Accutane) is the most effective long-term treatment.
Oral antibiotics reduce bacterial load during flare-ups.
Nodular Acne: Intralesional steroid injections for painful nodules.

general: The image quality was too low for a confident assessment.
Try again with better lighting and a clearer photo.
Make sure your face is centered and well-lit.
If problems persist, consult a dermatologist in person.
